title: Economy 10 explained, and how it differs from Economy 7
description: Economy 10 gives ten off-peak hours split across the day and night. How it works, how it differs from Economy 7, and whether you can still get it in 2026.
blurb: Ten off-peak hours split across day and night: how Economy 10 works, who it suits, and how it compares with Economy 7.
lede: Economy 10 is one of Britain's older multi-rate electricity tariffs, and one of the least understood. Where its better-known cousin Economy 7 gives you seven cheap hours in a single overnight block, Economy 10 spreads roughly ten cheap hours across three windows: a long stretch overnight, a top-up in the afternoon, and a shorter block in the evening. That pattern was designed for all-electric homes, where storage heaters that charged at midnight have run cold by teatime. It still exists in 2026, but only just, and whether it makes sense for you comes down to one question: how much of your electricity can you genuinely push into those cheap windows?
short_answer: Economy 10 gives around ten hours of cheaper electricity split into three blocks, typically overnight, in the afternoon and in the early evening, while Economy 7 gives seven continuous overnight hours. Off-peak units are priced well below the standard capped rate of 26.11p per kWh, but day units cost more than a single-rate tariff, so you need a large share of your usage inside the cheap windows to come out ahead. It mainly suits all-electric homes with storage or electric heating that needs a daytime top-up. Few suppliers offer it to new customers in 2026, and smart time-of-use tariffs are gradually replacing it.

sections.html:
Economy 10 is a multi-rate electricity tariff paired with a multi-register meter. The meter records your usage in separate registers, one clocking up units during the cheap off-peak windows and one during peak hours, and your supplier bills each register at a different price. You do not switch anything on or off yourself; the meter changes rate automatically at the set times.

The defining feature is the shape of the cheap hours. A typical Economy 10 pattern looks something like this, though exact times vary by region, supplier and meter:

  • Around five hours overnight, for example midnight to 5am
  • Around three hours in the afternoon, for example 1pm to 4pm
  • Around two hours in the evening, for example 8pm to 10pm

The times are set by your meter configuration, not chosen by you, and some meters run on local time while others stay on GMT all year, so the windows can shift by an hour in summer. The only reliable way to know your own windows is to ask your supplier or check your meter's programme. If you are not sure which register is which, our guide to reading your energy meter walks through multi-register displays.

The split-window design exists for one reason: electric heating. A storage heater charged overnight slowly leaks its heat through the day, and in a poorly insulated home it can be lukewarm by late afternoon. The afternoon and evening windows let heaters, electric boilers and hot water cylinders take a cheap top-up exactly when the overnight charge is fading.

sections.h2: Economy 10 vs Economy 7, side by side
sections.html:
Both tariffs work the same way mechanically: a multi-register meter, a cheap rate and a dear rate. The difference is when the cheap rate applies, and that changes who each tariff suits.

Economy 7Economy 10
Off-peak hours7 hoursAbout 10 hours
PatternOne continuous overnight block, often somewhere between 11pm and 8amThree blocks: overnight, afternoon and evening
Designed forStorage heaters and overnight hot waterElectric heating that needs daytime top-ups, electric boilers, underfloor heating
Day rateHigher than a single-rate tariffHigher still, reflecting the longer off-peak period
Availability in 2026Widely available, covered by its own price cap ratesLegacy only with most suppliers, rarely sold to new customers

Window times are illustrative; your meter sets the actual hours, which vary by region and supplier.

In practice the choice was never really between the two. Homes with modern, well-insulated storage heaters that hold their charge all day do fine on Economy 7. Homes with older heaters, direct-acting electric radiators or wet electric systems benefit from Economy 10's daytime windows, because they would otherwise be paying the punishing peak rate to stay warm in the evening.

There is a trade-off hiding in the longer off-peak period. Suppliers recover the cost of those ten cheap hours by setting the day rate higher than the Economy 7 equivalent. If a chunk of your usage still lands in peak hours, that premium eats the savings quickly.

sections.h2: What the cheap hours are actually worth
sections.html:
Multi-rate tariffs only pay off if enough of your usage sits inside the cheap windows. The arithmetic is simple, and worth doing with your own rates rather than anyone's averages.

Take an all-electric home using 20 kWh a day for heating and hot water through the colder months. Here is what that heating load costs at different unit rates, so you can see what moving it off-peak is worth:

Unit rate paidCost per day (20 kWh)Cost over a 150-day heating season
26.11p (standard single rate)£5.22£783
20p£4.00£600
15p£3.00£450
12p£2.40£360

Top row at 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. The other rows show the same usage at example off-peak rates; substitute the off-peak rate printed on your own bill.

If your off-peak rate is around 15p and you can run all 20 kWh of heating inside the cheap windows, you save over £300 a season against paying the standard rate. That is the whole case for Economy 10 in one number.

Now the other side of the ledger. Every unit you use at the peak rate costs more than the 26.11p you would pay on a single-rate tariff, sometimes considerably more. As a rule of thumb, you want at least 40 to 50 per cent of your total electricity inside the off-peak windows before a multi-rate tariff beats a single rate. With electric heating and hot water that is realistic. In a gas-heated home it almost never is. Your bill shows the split between registers, and our guide to understanding your energy bill explains where to find it.

sections.h2: Who Economy 10 suits, and who it does not
sections.html:
Economy 10 makes sense for a fairly narrow group of homes:

  • All-electric homes with older storage heaters. If your heaters run cold by evening, the afternoon and evening windows let them recharge cheaply rather than forcing you onto peak-rate panel heaters. Our guide to storage heaters covers how the charge-and-release cycle works.
  • Electric boilers and wet electric central heating. These cannot store a full day's heat overnight, so they genuinely need cheap daytime hours.
  • Electric underfloor heating in screed floors, which behaves like a big storage heater and can be charged in the off-peak blocks.
  • Homes heating water with an immersion heater. A well-lagged cylinder heated in the cheap windows covers most of the day; see our immersion heater cost guide for the sums.

It is a poor fit for:

  • Gas-heated homes. Your electricity use is mostly lights, appliances and electronics, spread across the day. The higher peak rate will cost you more than the cheap windows save.
  • Low electricity users. The savings scale with the kWh you can shift. If you use 6 to 8 kWh a day, there is not enough to shift.
  • Anyone who cannot move usage. If the washing, dishwasher, cooking and hot water all happen in peak hours, the tariff works against you.

sections.h2: Can you still get Economy 10 in 2026?
sections.html:
Barely. Most large suppliers stopped selling Economy 10 to new customers years ago and now run it as a legacy arrangement for households that already have the metering. You will not find it on comparison sites, and if you move into a home with an Economy 10 meter, your options are usually to stay on the incumbent supplier's multi-rate deal, ask which suppliers will take the meter on, or have the meter exchanged.

Two things are pushing the tariff towards retirement. First, the Radio Teleswitch Service, the longwave radio signal that told many older multi-register meters when to change rate, began a phased shutdown on 30 June 2025. Suppliers have been migrating affected households onto smart meters, which can replicate multi-rate billing without the radio signal. If you have an RTS meter and have not been contacted, chase your supplier, because a dead switching signal can leave heating or hot water stuck on or off.

Second, smart meters have made the whole category look dated. A smart meter can support any rate pattern a supplier wants to offer, which has produced a generation of time-of-use tariffs with cheap windows, half-hourly pricing or rates aimed at specific loads like EV charging and storage heating. For most households who would once have wanted Economy 10, a modern smart tariff is now the better and more available answer, and switching supplier with a smart meter is far simpler than hunting for a legacy multi-register deal.

On pricing: multi-register tariffs are covered by the Ofgem price cap, but the cap works as an average across rates, so suppliers have freedom in how they split the peak and off-peak prices. Two suppliers honouring the same cap can offer quite different splits. Check the current cap rules and your regional rates on Ofgem's website, as both change every quarter.

Economy 10 is Economy 7 with the cheap hours rearranged: roughly ten of them, split across the night, afternoon and evening, designed for electric heating that cannot survive on a single overnight charge. If you already have it, an all-electric home and the discipline to run heating, hot water and big appliances inside the windows, it can still undercut a single-rate tariff by hundreds of pounds a year. If you are paying its inflated day rate while heating with gas, it is quietly costing you money and you should ask your supplier about moving to a single rate.

If you do not have it, do not chase it. The tariff is a legacy product, the meters that ran it are being retired along with the radio signal that controlled many of them, and smart time-of-use tariffs now do the same job with more flexibility and actual availability. Work out your off-peak share from your bill, then pick the tariff shape that matches how your home actually uses electricity.

faq.a: Not automatically. Economy 10 gives you more cheap hours, including daytime windows, but its peak rate is usually set higher to compensate. If your heating needs a daytime top-up, Economy 10 tends to win; if modern storage heaters hold their overnight charge all day, Economy 7 is usually the better deal. Compare the actual rates on offer and your own usage split.

faq.q: What times are the Economy 10 off-peak hours?
faq.a: There is no single national pattern. A common arrangement is around five hours overnight, three in the afternoon and two in the evening, but the exact times depend on your region, supplier and meter programme, and some meters stay on GMT all year. Ask your supplier for your specific windows rather than relying on a generic list.

faq.q: Can I have Economy 10 with a smart meter?
faq.a: Yes. Smart meters can record usage in multiple registers, so a supplier can bill an Economy 10 style pattern through one. This is how households on old Radio Teleswitch meters are being migrated. In practice, though, suppliers usually move smart meter customers onto their modern time-of-use tariffs rather than recreating Economy 10 exactly.

faq.q: Should I switch from Economy 10 to a single-rate tariff?
faq.a: Check your bill for the split between off-peak and peak units. If less than roughly 40 per cent of your usage is off-peak, the higher day rate is probably costing you more than the cheap windows save and a single rate will likely be cheaper. If you heat an all-electric home inside the windows, staying on a multi-rate tariff usually pays.

faq.q: Does the energy price cap apply to Economy 10?
faq.a: Yes, multi-register tariffs are covered, but the cap applies as an average across the rates rather than capping each rate separately, so suppliers set the peak and off-peak split differently. Rates also vary by region and change each quarter, so check the current figures on Ofgem's website.
